strengths: Evidence is perfectly type-matched to claims: causal claims (C3, C4, C5) are supported by ablation studies (E5, E6), generalization claims (C2, C6, C7) by cross-dataset evaluations, and improvement claims (C1) by strong baseline comparisons.
Human evaluation (E2) is appropriately used for perceptual quality and motion plausibility where automated metrics fall short.

Falsification criteria are highly specific, actionable, and directly tied to the metrics used in the experiments (e.g., FID/FVD thresholds, human eval win rates >50%, trajectory difference significance).
Criteria for causal claims explicitly state the expected degradation upon ablation, making them independently testable.
Claims are precisely bounded to the datasets and metrics evaluated (e.g., specifying nuScenes val for C1, zero-shot Waymo for C6).

Assumptions are explicitly documented in problem.md and concepts.md, including the heuristic nature of the 3-frame position/velocity/acceleration prior and the boundary conditions of the reward function.
suggestions: Consider explicitly adding a 'Limitations' section in the main paper text (if not already present) to reiterate the heuristic nature of the 3-frame kinematic assumption and the relative-only nature of the reward function.

strengths: The logical arc from Observations to Gaps to Insights to Solutions is exceptionally clear and tightly coupled.

Every identified gap (uniform loss, structure degradation, single-frame incoherence) is directly addressed by a specific architectural or loss design (dynamic loss, structure loss, latent replacement) and verified by dedicated experiments.
The exploration tree captures genuine dead-ends (e.g., X1: single-frame prior insufficiency, X2: visual corruptions when freezing UNet without LoRA) with clear failure modes and lessons learned.
The pivot (P1) from external perception-based rewards to internal conditional variance is well-justified by the generalization bottleneck of external detectors.
Comprehensive baseline coverage across both domain-specific driving models and general video generation models.
Rigorous human evaluation protocol (Two-Alternative Forced Choice, 33 participants, 2640 responses) with standardized video preprocessing.
Clear hardware and training schedule specifications (two-stage resolution scaling, LoRA rank, etc.).
weaknesses: Ablation studies for the dynamic enhancement loss and structure preservation loss (E5) rely exclusively on qualitative visual comparisons. While common in video generation, this lacks the quantitative rigor seen in the rest of the paper.
suggestions: Introduce quantitative metrics for the E5 ablations, such as optical flow error specifically masked on dynamic foreground objects for C3, and edge-preservation scores (e.g., Laplacian variance or SSIM on high-frequency bands) for C4.

findings.observation: Ablation studies for the dynamic enhancement loss and structure preservation loss (E5) rely exclusively on qualitative visual comparisons. While acceptable in generative modeling, quantitative metrics specific to motion (e.g., masked optical flow error) or structure (e.g., high-frequency SSIM) would significantly strengthen the causal claims (C3, C4).

The assumption that 3 history frames perfectly map to position, velocity, and acceleration is correctly identified as a qualitative heuristic in the concepts file. Ensuring this nuance is explicitly highlighted in the main paper's limitation section will prevent over-interpretation by readers.
